1:
"But speak thou the things which become sound doctrine:" The witnesses note the charge to teach what befits sound doctrine. Gill notes the things which become sound doctrine are a good life and conversation, the various duties incumbent on professors of religion according to their different station, age, and sex, which are set out in the verses following. The witnesses note Paul charging Titus to speak the things which become sound doctrine; the call to teach the practical duties that adorn the gospel — that Paul, having exposed the false teachers (ch.1), turns to Titus's own ministry with a "but" of contrast: in opposition to the empty talkers, "speak thou the things which become sound doctrine" — that is, teach not idle fables but the wholesome truths and the holy duties that fittingly accompany and adorn the sound doctrine of the gospel — so that Titus's preaching is to join right doctrine to right living, setting out the duties suited to each class of believer; speak thou the things which become sound doctrine. The witnesses note but speak thou. The witnesses note the things which become sound doctrine.

The witnesses note the charge to teach what befits sound doctrine — not idle fables but the holy duties that adorn the gospel, suited to each class of believer.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to teach practical, becoming duties. The lesson the expositors draw is that Titus is to teach the holy duties that fittingly accompany sound doctrine. The witnesses note the but: "but speak thou" — Paul turns from false teachers to Titus's ministry; the but; the contrast; the Titus; the the ministry; the opposed. The expositors observe the speak: "speak thou" — teach and proclaim; the speak; the teach; the proclaim; the the ministry; the preach. The witnesses press the become: "the things which become sound doctrine" — what fittingly accompanies sound doctrine; the become; the fitting; the accompanies; the adorns; the suited. The expositors draw the lesson on the duties: Gill notes the duties of each station, age, and sex; the the duties; the each station; the age; the sex; the practical. The witnesses observe the right living: right doctrine is joined to right living; the right living; the right doctrine; the joined; the holy; the practical. The expositors note the adorn the gospel: the duties adorn the gospel; the adorn the gospel; the the duties; the becoming; the holy; the the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but speak thou, the the things which become sound doctrine, and the call to teach duties. The verse thus records the call to teach practical, becoming duties — teaching that Titus is to teach the holy duties that fittingly accompany sound doctrine.
2:
"That the aged men be sober, grave, temperate, sound in faith, in charity, in patience." The witnesses note the virtues required of aged men. Chrysostom notes there are failings which age has that youth has not — slowness, timidity, forgetfulness, insensibility, and irritability — and for this reason Paul exhorts old men to be vigilant. The witnesses note Paul directing that the aged men be sober, grave, temperate, and sound in faith, charity, and patience; the graces fitting for old age — that Paul begins with "the aged men," prescribing the virtues that should adorn their years: "sober" (vigilant and self-restrained, watchful against the failings of age), "grave" (dignified and serious in demeanour), "temperate" (discreet and moderate), and, in the chief Christian graces, "sound" — sound "in faith" (firm and unshaken in believing), "in charity" (in love), and "in patience" (in steadfast endurance) — so that the old men of the church are to be examples of a ripe, settled, and healthy godliness in the three abiding graces; sound in faith, in charity, in patience. The witnesses note that the aged men be sober, grave, temperate. The witnesses note sound in faith, in charity, in patience.

The witnesses note the graces of aged men — sober, grave, temperate, and sound in faith, charity, and patience; examples of a ripe, settled, healthy godliness.

So the witnesses make the verse the graces fitting for old age. The lesson the expositors draw is that the aged men are to be examples of a sound, settled godliness in faith, love, and patience. The witnesses note the sober: "that the aged men be sober" — vigilant, watchful against the failings of age; the sober; the vigilant; the watchful; the the failings; the self-restrained. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes age's failings — slowness, forgetfulness, irritability; the age's failings; the slowness; the forgetfulness; the irritability; the vigilant. The witnesses press the grave and temperate: "grave, temperate" — dignified and discreet; the grave; the temperate; the dignified; the serious; the moderate. The expositors draw the lesson on the sound in faith: "sound in faith" — firm and unshaken in believing; the sound in faith; the firm; the unshaken; the believing; the healthy. The witnesses observe the charity and patience: "in charity, in patience" — in love and steadfast endurance; the charity; the patience; the love; the endurance; the steadfast. The expositors note the ripe godliness: the old men are examples of a ripe, settled godliness; the ripe godliness; the settled; the examples; the healthy; the the graces. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that the aged men be sober grave temperate, the sound in faith in charity in patience, and the graces of old age. The verse thus records the graces fitting for old age — teaching that the aged men are to be examples of a sound, settled godliness in faith, love, and patience.
3:
"The aged women likewise, that they be in behaviour as becometh holiness, not false accusers, not given to much wine, teachers of good things;" The witnesses note the virtues required of aged women. Gill notes "the aged women" are not persons in office, ancient widows or presbyteresses, but elderly women generally, who are to be spoken to about what becomes their profession. The witnesses note Paul directing that the aged women be in behaviour as becometh holiness, not false accusers, not given to much wine, teachers of good things; the holy conduct fitting for older women — that Paul, "likewise," prescribes for the aged women the graces suited to them: that they be "in behaviour as becometh holiness" (their whole demeanour reverent and seemly, as befits a holy calling), "not false accusers" (not slanderers or talebearers), "not given to much wine" (not slaves to drink), and "teachers of good things" — so that, rather than gossiping or tippling, the older women are to be reverent in bearing and to use their experience in teaching what is good, especially to the younger women; not false accusers, not given to much wine, teachers of good things. The witnesses note that they be in behaviour as becometh holiness. The witnesses note not false accusers, not given to much wine, teachers of good things.

The witnesses note the holy conduct of aged women — reverent in behaviour, not slanderers, not given to wine, but teachers of good things, using their experience well.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy conduct fitting for older women. The lesson the expositors draw is that the aged women are to be reverent, free of slander and drink, and teachers of good. The witnesses note the becometh holiness: "that they be in behaviour as becometh holiness" — Gill notes their demeanour reverent, as befits holiness; the becometh holiness; the reverent; the seemly; the the demeanour; the holy. The expositors observe the not false accusers: "not false accusers" — not slanderers or talebearers; the not false accusers; the slanderers; the talebearers; the not; the gossip. The witnesses press the not given to wine: "not given to much wine" — not slaves to drink; the not given to wine; the not drink; the not slaves; the temperate; the sober. The expositors draw the lesson on the teachers of good: "teachers of good things" — using their experience to teach what is good; the teachers of good; the experience; the teach; the good things; the the younger. The witnesses observe the rather than gossip: rather than gossiping or tippling, they teach good; the rather than gossip; the not tippling; the teach good; the reverent; the good. The expositors note the the younger women: especially teaching the younger women; the the younger women; the teaching; the experience; the the younger; the good. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that they be in behaviour as becometh holiness, the not false accusers not given to much wine teachers of good things, and the holy conduct. The verse thus records the holy conduct fitting for older women — teaching that the aged women are to be reverent, free of slander and drink, and teachers of good.
4:
"That they may teach the young women to be sober, to love their husbands, to love their children," The witnesses note the older women's task of teaching the young women. Gill notes the aged women are to teach the young women to be sober — chaste, modest, and temperate, wise and prudent in their conduct toward their husbands and in the management of family affairs. The witnesses note Paul directing that the older women teach the young women to be sober, to love their husbands and their children; the older women's role in training the young in home and family — that Paul gives the great use of the older women's teaching of good things (v.3): "that they may teach the young women" — instructing them by word and example in their proper duties — beginning with the home: "to be sober" (discreet and self-controlled), "to love their husbands" (to be affectionate and faithful wives) and "to love their children" (to be tender, devoted mothers) — so that the experienced women are to train the younger in the foundational graces of a godly wife and mother; to love their husbands, to love their children. The witnesses note that they may teach the young women to be sober. The witnesses note to love their husbands, to love their children.

The witnesses note the older women's task — to train the young women to be sober, to love their husbands and children; the experienced teaching the foundational home graces.

So the witnesses make the verse the training of young women in home graces. The lesson the expositors draw is that the older women are to teach the young the duties of a godly wife and mother. The witnesses note the teach the young: "that they may teach the young women" — instructing them by word and example; the teach the young; the instruct; the by example; the the younger; the train. The expositors observe the sober: "to be sober" — discreet and self-controlled; the sober; the discreet; the self-controlled; the temperate; the modest. The witnesses press the love husbands: "to love their husbands" — affectionate, faithful wives; the love husbands; the affectionate; the faithful; the wives; the devoted. The expositors draw the lesson on the love children: "to love their children" — tender, devoted mothers; the love children; the tender; the devoted; the mothers; the children. The witnesses observe the home graces: these are the foundational graces of home and family; the home graces; the foundational; the home; the family; the wife and mother. The expositors note the by experience: the experienced women train the younger; the by experience; the experienced; the train; the the younger; the teach. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that they may teach the young women to be sober, the to love their husbands to love their children, and the training in home graces. The verse thus records the training of young women in home graces — teaching that the older women are to teach the young the duties of a godly wife and mother.
5:
"To be discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good, obedient to their own husbands, that the word of God be not blasphemed." The witnesses note the further virtues of the young women. Barnes notes "discreet" is the same word rendered "temperate" in v.2 and applied to the young women, and "chaste" means pure in heart and life. The witnesses note Paul directing that the young women be discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good, obedient to their husbands, that the word of God be not blasphemed; the household virtues that guard the gospel's good name — that Paul completes the list of graces the young women are to learn: "discreet" (sober-minded, self-controlled), "chaste" (pure in heart and body), "keepers at home" (diligent in the care and management of their households), "good" (kind and benevolent), and "obedient to their own husbands" (in due subjection to their own husbands) — and he names the motive that crowns all, "that the word of God be not blasphemed," lest the gospel be reproached through the ill conduct of Christian wives; that the word of God be not blasphemed. The witnesses note to be discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good, obedient to their own husbands. The witnesses note that the word of God be not blasphemed.

The witnesses note the household virtues of young women — discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good, obedient to their husbands — that the word of God be not blasphemed.

So the witnesses make the verse the household virtues that guard the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is that the young women's home graces are crowned by the motive that the gospel not be reproached. The witnesses note the discreet and chaste: "to be discreet, chaste" — Barnes notes sober-minded and pure; the discreet; the chaste; the sober-minded; the pure; the self-controlled. The expositors observe the keepers at home: "keepers at home" — diligent in managing their households; the keepers at home; the diligent; the managing; the the household; the care. The witnesses press the good and obedient: "good, obedient to their own husbands" — kind, and in due subjection to their husbands; the good; the obedient; the kind; the subjection; the their husbands. The expositors draw the lesson on the the motive: "that the word of God be not blasphemed" — the crowning motive; the the motive; the the word of God; the not blasphemed; the the crown; the the aim. The witnesses observe the gospel reproached: lest the gospel be reproached through ill conduct; the gospel reproached; the ill conduct; the the wives; the the gospel; the reproach. The expositors note the guard the name: the home virtues guard the gospel's good name; the guard the name; the the gospel; the good name; the the virtues; the guard. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to be discreet chaste keepers at home good obedient to their own husbands, the that the word of God be not blasphemed, and the household virtues. The verse thus records the household virtues that guard the gospel — teaching that the young women's home graces are crowned by the motive that the gospel not be reproached.
6:
"Young men likewise exhort to be sober minded." The witnesses note the brief charge to the young men. Gill notes this — to exhort the young men to be temperate, chaste, modest, moderate, wise, and prudent in all things — is said to Titus as his own province, as it was proper for aged women to teach the young women. The witnesses note Paul directing that the young men likewise be exhorted to be sober minded; the one great grace pressed on the young — self-control — that Paul, turning to the young men, sums their duty in a single comprehensive word: "exhort" them "to be sober minded" — that is, to be self-controlled, temperate, discreet, and prudent in all things; for the chief danger and the chief need of youth is the governing of its passions and appetites, and so the one grace of a sound, sober mind is pressed upon them, this being Titus's own charge to deliver; young men likewise exhort to be sober minded. The witnesses note young men likewise exhort. The witnesses note to be sober minded.

The witnesses note the brief charge to young men — to be sober minded; the one grace of self-control pressed on youth, whose chief need is the governing of its passions.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to the young to be sober minded. The lesson the expositors draw is that the young men are to be exhorted to self-control, the chief grace for youth. The witnesses note the young men: "young men likewise exhort" — Paul turns to the young men; the young men; the exhort; the the young; the likewise; the Titus's charge. The expositors observe Gill's note: Gill notes this is Titus's own province; the Titus's province; the his charge; the to exhort; the proper; the deliver. The witnesses press the sober minded: "to be sober minded" — self-controlled, temperate, prudent; the sober minded; the self-controlled; the temperate; the prudent; the discreet. The expositors draw the lesson on the one word: youth's whole duty summed in one comprehensive word; the one word; the comprehensive; the summed; the the duty; the sober. The witnesses observe the chief danger: the chief danger of youth is its passions; the chief danger; the youth; the passions; the appetites; the govern. The expositors note the chief need: self-control is youth's chief need; the chief need; the self-control; the youth; the the grace; the sober. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the young men likewise exhort, the to be sober minded, and the call to self-control. The verse thus records the call to the young to be sober minded — teaching that the young men are to be exhorted to self-control, the chief grace for youth.
7:
"In all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works: in doctrine shewing uncorruptness, gravity, sincerity," The witnesses note the charge that Titus himself be a pattern of good works. Gill notes it was not enough for Titus, nor any gospel minister, to deliver sound doctrine and exhort others, but he should through the whole of his conversation be a pattern of every good work. The witnesses note Paul charging Titus to show himself in all things a pattern of good works, and in his doctrine to show uncorruptness, gravity, and sincerity; the minister's own life and teaching as a model — that Paul presses Titus to back his teaching with his own example: "in all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works" (a living model that others may copy, his life adorning what he preaches), and that his teaching itself be marked by "uncorruptness" (purity, free from error and corrupt motive), "gravity" (a seriousness and dignity befitting holy things), and "sincerity" (honesty and integrity, no guile) — so that the minister is to preach as much by his life and the character of his teaching as by his words; in all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works. The witnesses note in all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works. The witnesses note in doctrine shewing uncorruptness, gravity, sincerity.

The witnesses note the charge that Titus be a pattern of good works — his life adorning his teaching, his doctrine marked by uncorruptness, gravity, and sincerity.

So the witnesses make the verse the minister's own life and teaching as a model. The lesson the expositors draw is that Titus must back his teaching with an exemplary life and pure, grave, sincere doctrine. The witnesses note the pattern: "in all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works" — Gill notes a living model others may copy; the pattern; the a model; the copy; the good works; the example. The expositors observe the life adorns: his life is to adorn what he preaches; the life adorns; the his life; the his preaching; the adorns; the example. The witnesses press the uncorruptness: "in doctrine shewing uncorruptness" — purity, free from error and corrupt motive; the uncorruptness; the purity; the no error; the no corrupt motive; the clean. The expositors draw the lesson on the gravity: "gravity" — seriousness and dignity befitting holy things; the gravity; the seriousness; the dignity; the holy things; the grave. The witnesses observe the sincerity: "sincerity" — honesty and integrity, no guile; the sincerity; the honesty; the integrity; the no guile; the sincere. The expositors note the preach by life: the minister preaches by his life as by his words; the preach by life; the his life; the his words; the by example; the model. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the in all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works, the in doctrine shewing uncorruptness gravity sincerity, and the minister as model. The verse thus records the minister's own life and teaching as a model — teaching that Titus must back his teaching with an exemplary life and pure, grave, sincere doctrine.
8:
"Sound speech, that cannot be condemned; that he that is of the contrary part may be ashamed, having no evil thing to say of you." The witnesses note the call to sound, blameless speech that silences opponents. Gill notes in the public ministry the wholesome words of the Lord Jesus should be used, the doctrines of the gospel expressed as near as can be in the words the Holy Ghost teaches, not in the enticing words of man's wisdom. The witnesses note Paul charging that Titus use sound speech that cannot be condemned, so that the opponent may be ashamed, having no evil to say; the unimpeachable speech that puts adversaries to shame — that Paul completes the charge to Titus's teaching with a word about his speech: it must be "sound speech, that cannot be condemned" (wholesome, true, and so blameless that no just fault can be found in it), and to a clear end — "that he that is of the contrary part may be ashamed, having no evil thing to say of you," that the gospel's adversaries, finding nothing to censure in his doctrine or life, might be put to silence and shame; so that a blameless ministry is itself a defence against the enemies of the truth; sound speech, that cannot be condemned. The witnesses note sound speech, that cannot be condemned. The witnesses note that he that is of the contrary part may be ashamed, having no evil thing to say of you.

The witnesses note the call to sound, unimpeachable speech — so that opponents, finding nothing to censure, may be put to shame; a blameless ministry a defence against the enemy.

So the witnesses make the verse the unimpeachable speech that shames adversaries. The lesson the expositors draw is that Titus's blameless speech is to leave the gospel's enemies nothing to censure, putting them to shame. The witnesses note the sound speech: "sound speech, that cannot be condemned" — Gill notes wholesome words, blameless; the sound speech; the wholesome; the blameless; the true; the no fault. The expositors observe the the Lord's words: the doctrines expressed in the Spirit-taught words, not man's wisdom; the the Lord's words; the Spirit-taught; the not man's wisdom; the the gospel; the doctrine. The witnesses press the ashamed: "that he that is of the contrary part may be ashamed" — the opponent put to shame; the ashamed; the the opponent; the the contrary part; the shame; the silenced. The expositors draw the lesson on the no evil to say: "having no evil thing to say of you" — finding nothing to censure; the no evil to say; the nothing to censure; the no fault; the silenced; the blameless. The witnesses observe the a defence: a blameless ministry is a defence against the enemy; the a defence; the blameless ministry; the the enemy; the the truth; the guard. The expositors note the put to silence: the adversaries are put to silence and shame; the put to silence; the the adversaries; the shame; the silenced; the no fault. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sound speech that cannot be condemned, the that he that is of the contrary part may be ashamed having no evil thing to say of you, and the unimpeachable speech. The verse thus records the unimpeachable speech that shames adversaries — teaching that Titus's blameless speech is to leave the gospel's enemies nothing to censure.
9:
"Exhort servants to be obedient unto their own masters, and to please them well in all things; not answering again;" The witnesses note the charge that servants obey and please their masters. Gill notes servants are to be obedient to their own masters — and not others — whether believers or unbelievers, gentle or froward; all their lawful commands ought to be obeyed, and they should endeavour to please them well in all things. The witnesses note Paul charging that servants be obedient to their own masters, please them well in all things, and not answer again; the obedience and good temper required of Christian servants — that Paul, turning to servants (a large class in the early church), bids Titus exhort them to a threefold duty: "to be obedient unto their own masters" (yielding due submission in all lawful things), "to please them well in all things" (seeking by diligent, willing service to give satisfaction), and "not answering again" (not contradicting, talking back, or replying with insolence) — so that the Christian servant is to be marked by ready obedience and a meek, respectful temper; obedient unto their own masters... not answering again. The witnesses note exhort servants to be obedient unto their own masters. The witnesses note to please them well in all things; not answering again.

The witnesses note the charge to servants — obedient to their masters, pleasing them well, not answering again; ready obedience joined to a meek, respectful temper.

So the witnesses make the verse the obedience and good temper of servants. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Christian servant is to obey, please, and not talk back to his master. The witnesses note the obedient: "exhort servants to be obedient unto their own masters" — Gill notes obedience in all lawful things; the obedient; the own masters; the lawful; the submission; the obey. The expositors observe the their own: their own masters, not others, believing or not; the their own; the not others; the believing or not; the gentle or froward; the masters. The witnesses press the please well: "to please them well in all things" — by diligent, willing service; the please well; the in all things; the diligent; the willing; the satisfaction. The expositors draw the lesson on the not answering: "not answering again" — not contradicting or talking back; the not answering; the not contradict; the not talk back; the no insolence; the meek. The witnesses observe the meek temper: a meek, respectful temper; the meek temper; the respectful; the meek; the temper; the humble. The expositors note the ready obedience: marked by ready obedience; the ready obedience; the ready; the obey; the willing; the submission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the exhort servants to be obedient unto their own masters, the to please them well in all things not answering again, and the obedience of servants. The verse thus records the obedience and good temper of servants — teaching that the Christian servant is to obey, please, and not talk back to his master.
10:
"Not purloining, but shewing all good fidelity; that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things." The witnesses note the charge to honesty and fidelity, that servants adorn the gospel. Gill notes "not purloining" is not stealing or embezzling their master's substance, not taking and using what is their property, nor keeping back part of money or goods committed to their trust. The witnesses note Paul charging that servants not purloin but show all good fidelity, that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour; the faithful honesty of servants that beautifies the gospel — that Paul completes the servant's duty: "not purloining" (not pilfering or secretly stealing from the master, nor embezzling what is entrusted) "but shewing all good fidelity" (proving themselves perfectly trustworthy in all that is committed to them) — and he crowns it with the highest motive: "that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things," that even the humblest believing slave, by honest and faithful service, might make the gospel beautiful and commend it to all; so that lowly fidelity is itself an ornament to the doctrine of Christ; that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things. The witnesses note not purloining, but shewing all good fidelity. The witnesses note that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things.

The witnesses note the faithful honesty of servants — not purloining but showing all good fidelity — that even the humblest believing slave might adorn the doctrine of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the fidelity that beautifies the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is that the servant's honest, faithful service adorns the doctrine of God our Saviour. The witnesses note the not purloining: "not purloining" — Gill notes not pilfering or embezzling the master's goods; the not purloining; the not stealing; the not embezzling; the not pilfering; the honest. The expositors observe the entrusted: not keeping back what is committed to their trust; the entrusted; the committed; the their trust; the not keeping back; the faithful. The witnesses press the all good fidelity: "but shewing all good fidelity" — proving perfectly trustworthy; the all good fidelity; the trustworthy; the faithful; the proven; the honest. The expositors draw the lesson on the adorn the doctrine: "that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour" — making the gospel beautiful; the adorn the doctrine; the the gospel; the beautiful; the commend; the ornament. The witnesses observe the humblest slave: even the humblest believing slave may adorn the gospel; the humblest slave; the believing; the the gospel; the adorn; the lowly. The expositors note the lowly fidelity: lowly fidelity is an ornament to Christ's doctrine; the lowly fidelity; the ornament; the Christ's doctrine; the beautify; the commend. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not purloining but shewing all good fidelity, the that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things, and the fidelity that beautifies. The verse thus records the fidelity that beautifies the gospel — teaching that the servant's honest, faithful service adorns the doctrine of God our Saviour.
11:
"For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men," The witnesses note the great motive — the saving grace of God appearing to all. Chrysostom notes that, having demanded such virtue even from servants — for it is great virtue to adorn the doctrine of God in all things — Paul adds the just cause why servants and all should be such: "for the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared." The witnesses note Paul declaring that the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared to all men; the saving grace of God now manifest as the ground of all holy living — that Paul gives the deep reason for all the foregoing duties: it is "the grace of God that bringeth salvation" — the free, saving favour of God, embodied and made visible in the gospel of Christ — which "hath appeared," shone forth like a sunrise upon the world, "to all men" (to men of every sort, Jew and Gentile, master and slave, not to one nation only); so that the very purpose and tendency of this manifested grace, as the next verses show, is to teach men to live holy lives — grace being not only the ground of salvation but the great school of godliness; the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men. The witnesses note the grace of God that bringeth salvation. The witnesses note hath appeared to all men.

The witnesses note the saving grace of God now appeared to all men — shining forth like a sunrise on men of every sort; grace the ground of all the holy duties enjoined.

So the witnesses make the verse the saving grace appearing as the ground of holiness. The lesson the expositors draw is that the grace of God, made manifest to all, is the deep reason and school for all holy living. The witnesses note the grace: "the grace of God that bringeth salvation" — the free, saving favour of God in the gospel; the grace; the saving; the favour; the the gospel; the free. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes grace is the just cause why even servants should be virtuous; the the cause; the servants; the virtuous; the grace; the the reason. The witnesses press the appeared: "hath appeared" — shone forth like a sunrise; the appeared; the shone forth; the sunrise; the manifest; the made visible. The expositors draw the lesson on the to all men: "to all men" — to men of every sort, Jew and Gentile, master and slave; the to all men; the every sort; the Jew and Gentile; the master and slave; the not one nation. The witnesses observe the ground of duties: the grace is the ground of all the foregoing duties; the ground of duties; the the duties; the the reason; the holy living; the ground. The expositors note the school of godliness: grace is the great school of godliness; the school of godliness; the teaches; the holy lives; the godliness; the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the grace of God that bringeth salvation, the hath appeared to all men, and the saving grace appeared. The verse thus records the saving grace appearing as the ground of holiness — teaching that the grace of God, made manifest to all, is the deep reason and school for all holy living.
12:
"Teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly, in this present world;" The witnesses note the school of grace, teaching denial of sin and a holy life. Gill notes grace teaches not all men to whom the gospel comes in its outward ministry — for many profess it who are never effectually taught by it — but those whom it influentially teaches it leads to deny sin and love holiness. The witnesses note Paul declaring that grace teaches us, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, to live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present world; the threefold holy life that the grace of God trains us to live — that Paul shows the very lesson the appeared grace teaches its true pupils: a twofold work of renunciation and pursuit — "denying ungodliness and worldly lusts" (renouncing all impiety toward God and all the sinful cravings of the world) and, on the positive side, that "we should live soberly, righteously, and godly" — "soberly" (in self-control toward ourselves), "righteously" (in justice toward others), and "godly" (in piety toward God) — and that "in this present world," here and now, amid its temptations; so that grace is no licence to sin but the very teacher of a complete and holy life; live soberly, righteously, and godly, in this present world. The witnesses note denying ungodliness and worldly lusts. The witnesses note we should live soberly, righteously, and godly, in this present world.

The witnesses note grace teaching its pupils to deny ungodliness and live soberly, righteously, and godly — toward self, others, and God; grace no licence but the school of holiness.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy life that grace trains us to live. The lesson the expositors draw is that grace teaches the denial of sin and a threefold holy life toward self, neighbour, and God. The witnesses note the teaching us: "teaching us" — Gill notes grace effectually teaches its true pupils; the teaching us; the effectually; the the pupils; the trains; the teaches. The expositors observe the denying: "that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts" — renouncing impiety and sinful cravings; the denying; the ungodliness; the worldly lusts; the renouncing; the impiety. The witnesses press the soberly: "we should live soberly" — in self-control toward ourselves; the soberly; the self-control; the toward ourselves; the temperate; the discreet. The expositors draw the lesson on the righteously and godly: "righteously, and godly" — in justice toward others, piety toward God; the righteously; the godly; the justice; the toward others; the piety toward God. The witnesses observe the present world: "in this present world" — here and now, amid temptation; the present world; the here and now; the amid temptation; the the world; the present. The expositors note the no licence: grace is no licence to sin but the teacher of holiness; the no licence; the not to sin; the the teacher; the holiness; the school. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, the we should live soberly righteously and godly in this present world, and the holy life grace trains. The verse thus records the holy life that grace trains us to live — teaching the denial of sin and a threefold holy life toward self, neighbour, and God.
13:
"Looking for that blessed hope, and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ;" The witnesses note the blessed hope of Christ's glorious appearing. Gill notes "that blessed hope" is not the grace of hope, which the saints already have, but rather Christ himself, the object and ground of hope, who is our hope, and the glory to be revealed at his appearing. The witnesses note Paul declaring that we are looking for that blessed hope and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ; the forward-looking hope fixed on Christ's glorious appearing and his deity — that Paul adds the upward and forward look that accompanies the holy life: while living godly now, the believer is "looking for that blessed hope" (waiting in joyful expectation for the blessed object of his hope) "and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ"; the witnesses note that this striking phrase, by its very construction, names Jesus Christ as "the great God and our Saviour" — a clear witness to the full deity of Christ, whose appearing in glory is the believer's blessed hope; the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ. The witnesses note looking for that blessed hope. The witnesses note the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the blessed hope — the glorious appearing of "the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ" — the phrase a clear witness to the full deity of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the blessed hope of Christ's glorious appearing. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer looks for Christ's appearing in glory, who is named the great God and our Saviour. The witnesses note the blessed hope: "looking for that blessed hope" — Gill notes Christ himself the object and ground of hope; the blessed hope; the Christ; the the object; the the ground; the waiting. The expositors observe the looking for: waiting in joyful expectation; the looking for; the waiting; the expectation; the joyful; the forward. The witnesses press the glorious appearing: "and the glorious appearing" — the appearing of Christ in glory; the glorious appearing; the the appearing; the in glory; the Christ; the revealed. The expositors present the deity of Christ: the witnesses note the phrase "the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ" names Jesus as the great God — a clear witness to his full deity. The witnesses draw the lesson on the great God: "the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ" — Christ named the great God and our Saviour; the the great God; the our Saviour; the Jesus Christ; the deity; the named. The expositors observe the full deity: this is a clear witness to the full deity of Christ; the full deity; the clear witness; the of Christ; the the great God; the divine. The witnesses note the blessed hope his appearing: Christ's appearing in glory is the believer's blessed hope; the his appearing; the in glory; the the blessed hope; the the believer; the hope. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the looking for that blessed hope, the the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ, and the deity of Christ. The verse thus records the blessed hope of Christ's glorious appearing — teaching that the believer looks for Christ's appearing in glory, who is the great God and our Saviour.
14:
"Who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all iniquity, and purify unto himself a peculiar people, zealous of good works." The witnesses note the self-giving of Christ to redeem and purify a people. Gill notes Christ gave not another, nor another's, but himself; not merely his own things, but his own self; not the world or its riches, not gold or silver, not the cattle on a thousand hills, not men nor angels, but himself, as the price of redemption. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ gave himself for us to redeem us from all iniquity and purify a peculiar people zealous of good works; the redeeming, purifying self-sacrifice of Christ and its purpose — that Paul grounds the blessed hope in the great work of the Saviour who is its object: he "gave himself for us" (his very self, in his atoning death, as the ransom-price), and that with a twofold purpose — "that he might redeem us from all iniquity" (deliver us by his blood from the guilt and dominion of all sin) and "purify unto himself a peculiar people" (cleanse for himself a people of his own special possession), "zealous of good works" — so that Christ died not only to forgive but to form a holy, eager, good-working people for his own; who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all iniquity. The witnesses note who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all iniquity. The witnesses note purify unto himself a peculiar people, zealous of good works.

The witnesses note Christ giving himself to redeem from all iniquity and purify a peculiar people zealous of good works — dying not only to forgive but to form a holy people.

So the witnesses make the verse the redeeming, purifying self-sacrifice of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ gave himself to redeem from all sin and form a holy, good-working people of his own. The witnesses note the gave himself: "who gave himself for us" — Gill notes Christ gave his very self as the ransom-price; the gave himself; the his very self; the the ransom; the for us; the the price. The expositors observe the not another: not another or another's, but himself; the not another; the himself; the his own self; the not riches; the the price. The witnesses press the redeem: "that he might redeem us from all iniquity" — deliver us from the guilt and dominion of all sin; the redeem; the from all iniquity; the the guilt; the the dominion; the deliver. The expositors draw the lesson on the purify: "and purify unto himself a peculiar people" — cleanse a people of his own special possession; the purify; the a peculiar people; the his own; the special possession; the cleanse. The witnesses observe the zealous: "zealous of good works" — eager and devoted to good works; the zealous; the good works; the eager; the devoted; the holy. The expositors note the not only forgive: Christ died not only to forgive but to form a holy people; the not only forgive; the to form; the a holy people; the good-working; the holy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who gave himself for us that he might redeem us from all iniquity, the purify unto himself a peculiar people zealous of good works, and the self-sacrifice of Christ. The verse thus records the redeeming, purifying self-sacrifice of Christ — teaching that Christ gave himself to redeem from all sin and form a holy people of his own.
15:
"These things speak, and exhort, and rebuke with all authority. Let no man despise thee." The witnesses note the charge to teach and rebuke with full authority. Gill notes "these things" are sound doctrine — the doctrines of grace, salvation, and redemption by Christ, of peace, pardon, and cleansing by his blood — which Titus is to speak out clearly, plainly, publicly, boldly, and faithfully. The witnesses note Paul charging Titus to speak, exhort, and rebuke these things with all authority, letting no man despise him; the authority with which the minister is to teach and the respect he is to command — that Paul closes the chapter by charging Titus to deliver all the foregoing — the sound doctrine and the holy duties — with full ministerial authority: "these things speak" (declare them), "and exhort" (urge them upon the people), "and rebuke" (reprove the disobedient) "with all authority" (in the full commission of Christ's minister), and adds, "let no man despise thee" — so to bear himself, in life and teaching, that none can rightly slight his ministry; so that the minister is to teach with boldness and to maintain the dignity of his office; these things speak, and exhort, and rebuke with all authority. The witnesses note these things speak, and exhort, and rebuke with all authority. The witnesses note let no man despise thee.

The witnesses note the charge to speak, exhort, and rebuke with all authority — letting no man despise him; the minister teaching with boldness and maintaining his office's dignity.

So the witnesses make the verse the authority of the minister's teaching. The lesson the expositors draw is that Titus is to deliver sound doctrine and rebuke with full authority, commanding due respect. The witnesses note the these things: "these things speak" — Gill notes the sound doctrine and duties; the these things; the sound doctrine; the the duties; the declare; the speak. The expositors observe the speak boldly: declare them clearly, plainly, boldly; the speak boldly; the clearly; the plainly; the boldly; the faithfully. The witnesses press the exhort and rebuke: "and exhort, and rebuke" — urge them on the people, reprove the disobedient; the exhort; the rebuke; the urge; the reprove; the the disobedient. The expositors draw the lesson on the all authority: "with all authority" — in the full commission of Christ's minister; the all authority; the full commission; the Christ's minister; the the office; the bold. The witnesses observe the despise not: "let no man despise thee" — so bear himself that none can slight his ministry; the despise not; the slight; the his ministry; the bear himself; the respect. The expositors note the dignity of office: the minister maintains the dignity of his office; the dignity of office; the the office; the maintain; the boldness; the respect. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the these things speak and exhort and rebuke with all authority, the let no man despise thee, and the authority of teaching. The verse thus records the authority of the minister's teaching — teaching that Titus is to deliver sound doctrine and rebuke with full authority, commanding due respect.
